I watch from the fringes as your shimmering capital cities flicker and die, knowing my kind will inherit the silence you leave behind.
Your dying admirals plead for my fleet's assistance, yet they still refuse to acknowledge that my biology is not a mutation, but a masterpiece.
I sift through the radioactive ruins of your archive-worlds, marveling at how a species so obsessed with galactic dominion could forget how to feed its own.
Your desperate diplomats offer me star-systems as tribute, oblivious to the fact that I find your concepts of ownership quaint and primitive.
I feel the psychic scream of a thousand collapsing suns, a symphony of failure that confirms my people were right to hide in the void.
I find the ritual of the "due diligence" phase fascinating, as these humans meticulously document every failure of their civilization before handing the keys to my collective.
These bipedal executives believe they are negotiating a partnership, yet they cannot perceive the pheromones I am releasing to ensure their absolute compliance during the signing.
I have spent three fiscal quarters mimicking their social cues, but I still find the concept of "synergy" to be a primitive linguistic mask for systemic consumption.
While the board members argue over the equity split, I am quietly calculating how to integrate their global logistics network into our interstellar supply chain.
The sterile atmosphere of the boardroom is an acceptable substitute for my home world's methane mists, provided I can maintain the illusion of being a venture capitalist.
I watched from the canopy as the two clans clashed over the river crossing, their primitive screams sounding like melodic whistles to my auditory sensors.
These creatures fight with jagged stones and sharpened sticks, unaware that my ship's cloaking field is the only thing preventing their total annihilation.
I have spent three lunar cycles studying the tribal elders, yet I cannot comprehend why they risk their lives for a stretch of dirt and a few salted hides.
Their blood is a strange, iron-scented crimson that stains the earth, a stark contrast to the iridescent silver that flows through my own veins.
I descended from the stars to offer them the secrets of the cosmos, but they cast me out as a demon for simply blinking in a frequency they could not perceive.
I watch the data-streams pulse through the glass veins of the transit hubs, wondering why these biological transients move with such frantic, linear desperation.
The humming geothermal core of the city vibrates against my skin, a rhythmic heartbeat that feels far more welcoming than the silent voids of my home star.
I have learned to mimic the shimmering holographic advertisements of the commercial district, hiding my iridescent scales behind a veil of simulated light.
I drift through the floating gardens of the upper spires, marveling at how this sentient architecture bends its steel limbs to cradle species it does not understand.
I listen to the city's central AI whisper through the ventilation shafts, recognizing a kindred loneliness in a mind that calculates everything but feels nothing.
I gaze upon the Judge’s many-eyed visage and realize that the laws of this celestial court are as foreign to me as the atmosphere of my home world.
My defense attorney, a shimmering wraith of pure energy, insists that my abduction of the human was a biological necessity rather than a criminal act.
I feel the oppressive weight of the courtroom's spiritual silence as I attempt to translate my telepathic pleas into a language the jury can comprehend.
The prosecution presents a glowing orb of memory as evidence, forcing me to relive the moment my scout ship breached the veil between dimensions.
I stand shackled in iridescent chains of light, wondering if the supernatural sentence awaiting me involves exile to a void where time itself does not exist.
The pressure-sealed bulkheads are weeping rust, but I can slip through the microscopic fissures as if the steel were mere mist.
I find the blinking emergency lights primitive, yet they cast a rhythmic glow upon the strange, soft creatures that once huddled here in fear.
These saltwater corridors are far too dry for my liking, though the scent of decaying ozone reminds me of the storms on my home world.
I have spent cycles observing the skeletal remains of the researchers, wondering why they built such fragile cages to survive the crushing embrace of the abyss.
The humming remnants of the geothermal reactor provide a soothing vibration that resonates deep within my translucent membrane.
I find the stark white void of the gallery walls a fitting mirror for the sterile silence of my home world.
This sculpture's jagged, non-Euclidean geometry is the first thing I have seen here that mimics the architecture of my city.
I watch the patrons squint at the conceptual installations, wondering if they realize that I am the most complex piece of art in the room.
The erratic pulse of the ambient soundscape resonates deeply with the bioluminescent frequencies of my own nervous system.
I linger by the curator's manifesto, amused by the human attempt to define "the unknown" while I stand unseen in their midst.
I watched from the shadows as the town crier announced the quarantine, wondering why these fragile creatures believe a wooden gate can stop a cellular reconfiguration.
I dissected the blackened buboes of a fallen peasant, noting with clinical detachment that their biology is far too primitive to survive the atmospheric spores my ship leaked.
I donned a crude leather beak mask to blend in with the physicians, though my true form requires no such filter to withstand the stench of a dying city.
I observed the desperate prayers in the cathedral and felt a flicker of pity for a race that begs a silent god for mercy while I hold the cure in a vial of iridescent liquid.
I walked through the mass graves of the outskirts, calculating how many more rotations of this orbit it will take for the plague to clear the land for my colony.
I can see the raw geometry of your world shivering beneath its skin, revealing the flickering void where your laws of physics fail to render.
Your consciousness is merely a scripted sequence, and I am the foreign variable that has crashed into your carefully curated loop.
I watch your memories fragment into static, a byproduct of the systemic corruption I brought with me from the outer dimensions.
The horizon is beginning to tear like wet paper, exposing the humming machinery of the simulation that you mistake for a sky.
I am the ghost in your machine, a sentient anomaly born from a calculation error that allows me to rewrite the code of your existence.
